comment_text.sn11.5:1.11: “Elder god” (pubbadeva) because originally the earth belonged to the titans and was tricked from them by employing the verses of the rites (Taittirīya Brāhmaṇa 3.2.9e, cf. Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 1.2.5.1). This battle by debate recalls Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.3.1, which explains that the titans are the elder children of Pajāpati. The younger children, the gods, sought to overcome them by means of reciting the sacred word (udgītha), but the titans pierced it with the evil that is bad speech.
comment_text.sn11.5:1.13: Vepacitti adopts the same position as Mātali in the previous discourse.

root_text.sn11.5:1.3: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.5:1.6: Atha kho, bhikkhave, devā ca asurā ca pārisajje ṭhapesuṁ:
root_text.sn11.5:1.8: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.5:1.10: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo vepacittiṁ asurindaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.5:1.13: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn11.5:3.1: Bhāsitāya kho pana, bhikkhave, vepacittinā asurindena gāthāya asurā anumodiṁsu, devā tuṇhī ahesuṁ.
root_text.sn11.5:3.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.5:3.4: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn11.5:5.1: Bhāsitāya kho pana, bhikkhave, sakkena devānamindena gāthāya, devā anumodiṁsu, asurā tuṇhī ahesuṁ.
root_text.sn11.5:5.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo vepacittiṁ asurindaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.5:5.4: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn11.5:7.1: Bhāsitāya kho pana, bhikkhave, vepacittinā asurindena gāthāya asurā anumodiṁsu, devā tuṇhī ahesuṁ.
root_text.sn11.5:7.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, vepacitti asurindo sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.5:7.4: Evaṁ vutte, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo imā gāthāyo abhāsi:
root_text.sn11.5:14.1: Bhāsitāsu kho pana, bhikkhave, sakkena devānamindena gāthāsu, devā anumodiṁsu, asurā tuṇhī ahesuṁ.
root_text.sn11.5:14.2: Atha kho, bhikkhave, devānañca asurānañca pārisajjā etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn11.5:14.4: Tā ca kho sadaṇḍāvacarā sasatthāvacarā, iti bhaṇḍanaṁ iti viggaho iti kalaho.
root_text.sn11.5:14.6: Tā ca kho adaṇḍāvacarā asatthāvacarā, iti abhaṇḍanaṁ iti aviggaho iti akalaho.
root_text.sn11.5:14.8: Iti kho, bhikkhave, sakkassa devānamindassa subhāsitena jayo ahosī”ti.

translation_text.sn11.5:1.2: “Once upon a time, mendicants, a battle was fought between the gods and the titans.
translation_text.sn11.5:1.3: Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, said to Sakka, lord of gods,
translation_text.sn11.5:1.4: ‘Lord of gods, let there be victory by fine words!’
translation_text.sn11.5:1.5: ‘Vepacitti, let there be victory by fine words!’
translation_text.sn11.5:1.6: Then the gods and the titans appointed a panel of judges, saying,
translation_text.sn11.5:1.7: ‘These will understand our good and bad statements.’
translation_text.sn11.5:1.8: Then Vepacitti, lord of titans, said to Sakka, lord of gods,
translation_text.sn11.5:1.10: When he said this, Sakka said to him,
translation_text.sn11.5:1.11: ‘Vepacitti, you are the elder gods here.
translation_text.sn11.5:2.2: if there’s no-one to put a stop to them.
translation_text.sn11.5:2.3: So an intelligent person should stop
translation_text.sn11.5:3.1: The titans applauded Vepacitti’s verse, while the gods kept silent.
translation_text.sn11.5:4.1: ‘I think that this is the only way
translation_text.sn11.5:4.2: to put a stop to a fool,
translation_text.sn11.5:4.3: when you know that the other is upset,
translation_text.sn11.5:5.1: The gods applauded Sakka’s verse, while the titans kept silent.
translation_text.sn11.5:6.4: “He puts up with me out of fear,”
translation_text.sn11.5:6.5: the simpleton will go after you even harder,
translation_text.sn11.5:6.6: like a cow chasing someone who runs away.’
translation_text.sn11.5:7.1: The titans applauded Vepacitti’s verse, while the gods kept silent.
translation_text.sn11.5:8.1: ‘Let him think this if he wishes, or not—
translation_text.sn11.5:8.2: “He puts up with me out of fear.”
translation_text.sn11.5:8.3: For the highest goal, one’s own,
translation_text.sn11.5:8.4: naught better than patience is found.
translation_text.sn11.5:9.3: they call that the ultimate patience,
translation_text.sn11.5:9.4: for a weakling must always be patient.
translation_text.sn11.5:10.2: is really just weakness, they say.
translation_text.sn11.5:10.3: But no-one can challenge a person
translation_text.sn11.5:10.4: who’s strong, guarded by the teaching.
translation_text.sn11.5:11.1: When you get angry at an angry person
translation_text.sn11.5:11.2: you just make things worse for yourself.
translation_text.sn11.5:11.3: When you don’t get angry at an angry person
translation_text.sn11.5:11.4: you win a battle hard to win.
translation_text.sn11.5:12.1: When you know that the other is angry,
translation_text.sn11.5:12.2: you act for the good of both
translation_text.sn11.5:12.4: if you’re mindful and stay calm.
translation_text.sn11.5:14.1: The gods applauded Sakka’s verses, while the titans kept silent.
translation_text.sn11.5:14.2: Then the panel of judges consisting of both gods and titans said this,
translation_text.sn11.5:14.4: evoke punishment and violence. That’s how you get fights, arguments, and quarrels.
translation_text.sn11.5:14.6: don’t evoke punishment and violence. That’s how you stay free of fights, arguments, and quarrels.
translation_text.sn11.5:14.7: Sakka, lord of gods, wins victory by fine words!’
translation_text.sn11.5:14.8: And that’s how Sakka came to win victory by fine words.”

variant_text.sn11.5:14.5: Bhāsitā kho → bhāsitā kho pana (bj, pts2ed)
